video_title: The Tokenmaxxing Party Is Over and No One Wants to Admit It
gist: Dan Nathan and Guy Adami argue the AI 'tokenmaxxing' boom is quietly inflecting: large cloud customers (Microsoft, Amazon, Uber) are slashing AI-token/Claude-code consumption after blowing through annual budgets in a quarter, which threatens the consumption-revenue narrative propping up OpenAI/Anthropic IPOs. They pair this with a broadly bearish macro read — record valuations, a strained lower-income consumer, sticky inflation, and parabolic moves in Dell/software/semis — and warn the S&P/NASDAQ could be cut in half within one to two years.

insights.claim: Model makers (OpenAI's GPT, Anthropic's Claude, Google's Gemini, xAI's Grok) shifted from flat all-you-can-eat subscription pricing to consumption-based token billing, which is inflating their reported revenue ahead of IPOs by making per-query usage monetizable rather than commoditized.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Consumption pricing is the load-bearing assumption behind AI-lab IPO valuations; if usage falls, the revenue story underneath those listings weakens.
insights.quote: now these companies are charging by consumption and it's making their this is anthropic and and open. It's making their numbers look a lot better into their IPOs.

insights.claim: Microsoft, Amazon, and Uber have all reported blowing through their entire annual AI-token/cloud budgets within the first months of the year and are now pulling back, signaling enterprise AI consumption may be rolling over rather than compounding.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Falling enterprise token consumption directly undercuts the usage-growth narrative supporting AI infrastructure and AI-lab valuations.
insights.quote: they've been blowing through their entire year budgets in a matter of months

insights.claim: Microsoft — which owns 27% of OpenAI, just invested $5 billion in Anthropic, and integrated both into Copilot as its key monetization unlock beyond cloud — is pulling its own engineers back from using Claude Code, a notable tell given Microsoft's deep AI-lab stakes.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: If the largest AI-lab backer is curbing its own token usage, the demand assumptions baked into Copilot monetization and AI-lab revenue look fragile.
insights.quote: They own 27% of it. They just invested $5 billion in anthropic... And they are pulling back their engineers from using clawed code.

insights.claim: Uber blew through its entire 2026 cloud-code/token budget within the first quarter and its CEO is explicitly citing a lack of ROI as the reason for pulling back AI spend.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: An explicit CEO 'no ROI' admission is harder evidence than budget overruns that the AI-productivity payoff is not yet materializing for buyers.
insights.quote: They blew through their entire 2026 budget for cloud code and for tokens within the first quarter of this year. They are pulling back. Their CEO is specifically talking about not getting the ROI.

insights.claim: 'Tokenmaxxing' — engineers competing on internal leaderboards to consume the most tokens as a proxy for productivity — is a Goodhart's-law failure: a cost metric was turned into a target and ceased to measure productivity, so the apparent consumption boom may not reflect real value created.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Frames the token-usage surge as partly gamed rather than organic demand, implying downside when companies optimize the metric away.
insights.quote: When a measure becomes a target it ceases to be a good measure.

insights.claim: The Jevons-paradox bull case for tokens (cheaper compute drives exponentially more usage, fueling the agent inflection) is being undercut in real time as the largest buyers cut consumption, suggesting efficiency gains may not produce the assumed runaway demand for AI labs.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Challenges the core demand thesis underpinning AI-infrastructure and AI-lab valuations as IPOs approach.
insights.quote: If that is being turned upside down guy, like then you're going to see consumption come down... If that goes away, it might question all of this.

insights.claim: The real inflection/test for the AI bubble will be how OpenAI, Anthropic, and xAI trade in the aftermarket after their IPOs, not in the pre-IPO private or public hype — that post-listing price action is where a market top would be signaled.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Tells investors to watch AI-lab post-IPO trading as the tell for the cycle top rather than pre-IPO sentiment.
insights.quote: The real tell is going to come in the form of how these things trade after the IPOs. That's when you can start hearing people talk about ringing a bell at the top.

insights.claim: Google/Alphabet is positioned to win the AI race by deploying Gemini across 13 platforms with over a billion users each without cannibalizing core Search, while also monetizing via GCP and selling TPUs as a second-source alternative to Nvidia GPUs.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Positions Alphabet as the structurally advantaged AI distribution and infrastructure play versus pure-play labs and Nvidia-dependent compute.

insights.claim: Cloud providers that hold equity stakes in AI labs while simultaneously cutting their own customers'/engineers' token usage create a contradiction that will surface more enterprise pullback disclosures like Uber's.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Signals more negative AI-consumption datapoints are likely coming, a forward catalyst for re-rating AI names lower.
insights.quote: If you're a cloud provider and you have these investments in these companies and you're pulling back on the usage of your own customers, you're sure as going to hear more about like the Ubers and the like.

themes.theme: AI hardware froth — Dell, memory, margin trap

insights.claim: Dell jumped 30% in one session (a ~$270B market cap) on a large AI-server number, is up nearly 300% from earlier-year lows, but is printing only an 18% gross margin versus the 23-24% it earned three years ago when AI-server demand was far lower — its margins are compressing, not expanding, even at peak demand.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Margin compression amid record demand flags that the AI-server boom is commoditized; the parabolic move is unlikely to be a durable three-year trend.
insights.quote: here's a company that just printed a 18% gross margin... three years ago, okay, when they were not had nearly the demand... they were basically printing 23 24% margins.

insights.claim: Server assemblers Dell and Super Micro are caught in a margin vise: capacity-constrained component suppliers (Micron, Nvidia, Intel, AMD, SanDisk, Western Digital) hold pricing power, so server makers' margins likely fall further unless they can raise prices in a commoditized business.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Frames the AI server-assembler trade as structurally margin-disadvantaged versus upstream component suppliers — a relative pair-trade signal.
insights.quote: if Micron and Nvidia and Intel and AMD and SanDisk and Western Digital have all this pricing power that can only weigh on their margins

insights.claim: Micron is expected to swing from a negative gross margin three years ago to a ~77% gross margin, the embodiment of the 'cyclical becomes secular' narrative driving memory stocks up ~1,000% — a framing the hosts treat skeptically as unlikely to persist.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: A 77% margin assumption in a historically cyclical memory business is a valuation/earnings risk if the cycle mean-reverts.
insights.quote: Micron had a negative margin just three years ago and now they're going to expect it to have a 77% gross margin. Cyclical becomes secular.

insights.claim: Sell-side analysts are badly behind the AI-hardware moves: the average of 29 analyst price targets on Dell was ~$230 while the stock traded north of $400, showing even specialist coverage failed to anticipate the rally.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Wide gap between targets and price signals momentum-driven mispricing and limited fundamental anchor on the upside.
insights.quote: the average price target for Dell, and I round it up, is about $230. And as you just mentioned, we just traded north of 400.

insights.claim: Dell historically trades at a discount to the market and should not command a market multiple; at ~$300 it was ~19.5x forward (reasonable), but at ~$400 valuation becomes a headwind, and this quarter is likely the best result Dell reports for some time.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Flags Dell as a peak-earnings, peak-multiple short/avoid candidate after the parabolic move.
insights.quote: my sense is is this one we just heard might be the best thing you hear from Dell in quite some time.

insights.claim: Parabolic 300%-in-two-months moves in hardware names like Dell are a bearish character signal, not a bullish one, and exiting only into strong up-days is the disciplined approach because round-trips down 15%+ feel far worse than profit-taking.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: A risk-management rule: take AI-hardware profits into strength rather than holding for momentum/narrative.
insights.quote: when you see stocks like this go absolutely parabolic... it it's just it's not bullish.

insights.claim: The software ETF IGV bounced from an April low near $73 to its ~99-100 target at the 200-day moving average (its first move back above the 200-day since the first week of the year), making it logical to take profits and look for a re-entry rather than chase.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Actionable level: software at the 200-day is a take-profit zone, but could attract flows if the semi trade stalls.
insights.quote: We had flagged sort of 99 because that was a 200 day moving average and here we are.

insights.claim: Oracle is up ~7.5% to a multi-month high and looks to have made a real technical bottom as the capital-raise overhang faded (after heavy debt issuance and a pledge not to raise more), potentially riding a tailwind into an OpenAI IPO.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Oracle re-rating off the lows is a lever on AI-capex sentiment and an OpenAI-IPO beneficiary trade.

insights.claim: Enterprise software is seeing indiscriminate level-buying: Palantir up ~10% (and ~7.5% the prior day for no specific reason as a laggard), ServiceNow up ~13%, and Salesforce up ~9% the second day after a mediocre quarter that initially left it unchanged.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Price action divorced from fundamentals (Salesforce up 9% on a soft quarter) signals momentum-driven, late-cycle buying in software.
insights.quote: Salesforce, which reported... wasn't a great quarter... Stock was literally unchanged the day after. Today, it's up 9%.

insights.claim: Michael Burry has been publicly 'picking at' enterprise software, adding a notable-investor signal to the software rotation theme.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: A contrarian-investor name attached to the space is a sentiment datapoint worth tracking.

insights.claim: Software is rallying hard while the semiconductor ETF SMH is still at all-time highs, meaning the software move is NOT funded by rotation out of semis — both are rising together, a sign of broad froth rather than a clean rotation.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Simultaneous melt-up in both software and semis warns of a liquidity-driven, fragile rally vulnerable to a joint unwind.
insights.quote: the SMH is still trading at all-time highs... So these software stocks have been able to do that without money really coming out in a meaningful way.

insights.claim: Presidential commentary singling out individual companies (positive remarks on Micron and Palantir) is acting as a tailwind, with investors unwilling to fight stocks the administration appears to favor.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Policy/jawboning risk premium: names mentioned favorably by the president carry a political bid that can distort fundamentals.
insights.quote: in a world where picking winners and losers and seemingly a track record to support it, people are not going to get in the way of this stuff.

insights.claim: The S&P 500 is near an all-time high around 7,600 with momentum toward 8,000, the dollar near 99, and the 10-year yield having spiked above 4.65% before easing, with markets in a 'see no evil' posture ignoring bad news.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Sets the late-cycle backdrop: all-time-high equities coexisting with elevated yields and a strong dollar, a tension that historically resolves badly.
insights.quote: S&P 500 basically at 7600, which is an all-time high... the market's sort of in the see no evil camp right now.

insights.claim: Former Treasury/Bessent adviser Joe Lavorgna argues the Fed should be ~100bp HIGHER — not cutting — because core PCE inflation is back near 4% while Fed funds sits below 4% (upper bound ~3.75%), a non-consensus hawkish view from someone seen as administration-aligned.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: An administration-adjacent strategist calling for hikes (against Trump's rate-cut wish) flags upside rate risk the market is ignoring.
insights.quote: the Fed fund should be 100 basis points higher... you got inflation that's back near 4% and you have a Fed funds that's below 4%.

insights.claim: Guy Adami's contrarian rates view: a Fed hiking cycle would be championed by the bond market — the front end rises on hikes but the long end (the 10-year everyone watches) could actually fall, re-inverting the curve and getting rates where the administration wants them.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Counterintuitive thesis: hikes could lower long-end yields — a setup for long-duration bonds if the Fed turns hawkish.
insights.quote: I can make an argument that the back end, which is what everybody wants to focus on... would actually go lower

insights.claim: The market has priced the next Fed rate cut all the way out to fall 2027, implying the Fed is largely irrelevant to current all-time-high pricing and is likely to simply stay the course with no pressure to act.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: If equities are at highs without needing cuts, monetary policy is not the marginal driver — rate-cut bets offer little upside catalyst.
insights.quote: the next rate cut is priced into the fall of 2027... the Fed maybe doesn't matter all that much.

insights.claim: Incoming Fed chair Kevin Warsh is framed as an independent reformist who won't be pushed around by the president, faces a hard first meeting on June 17th, and wants to reduce Fed communication with markets — adding policy-uncertainty risk.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: A reformist chair who communicates less injects unpredictability into rate expectations around the June 17 meeting.

insights.claim: Crude oil is sticky around $88-90 after the administration's Memorial Day commentary eased war/Iran tensions via a two-month ceasefire 'one-pager' that defers the hardest issues, yet equities are indifferent to the geopolitical outcome.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Oil holding ~$88 keeps an inflation/affordability overhang alive even as markets shrug off geopolitics.
insights.quote: Crude oil has been a bit sticky up here. It's trading at 88 right now.

insights.claim: The administration treats the stock market as a barometer of presidential popularity and has historically been spooked by the bond market, so a 10-year near 4.65% and a strong dollar are levels policymakers were actively focused on around Memorial Day.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Implies a policy 'put' tied to yields/equities — useful for anticipating rhetoric-driven reversals near stress points.
insights.quote: there was no way in the Memorial Day weekend the administration was going to let the market sort of teeter... with crude oil still above $100 a barrel and interest rates zooming higher.

insights.claim: The US consumer is weaker than the market believes: auto loan payments now average ~$1,000/month, gas hovers near $4.50/gallon, and 90-day-plus credit-card delinquencies are above 13% — the highest in 14-15 years.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Hard delinquency and cost-of-living data argue the consumer-spending leg of the bull case is cracking at the lower end.
insights.quote: credit card delinquencies, 90-day plus, north of 13%, the highest level we've seen in 14 or 15 years.

insights.claim: Costco fell ~12% in a straight line from a May 19 all-time high (~$1,096 to ~$954, at March-low support) and Walmart fell ~12-13% from its all-time high — both on valuation, not bad quarters, signaling these inflation-beneficiary 'trade-down' names were crowded trades.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Even quality defensives are being punished purely on valuation — a warning that multiple compression is starting at the safest end of the market.
insights.quote: These were meant to be the beneficiaries of inflation, the beneficiaries of a trade down by a consumer... were these two crowded trades

insights.claim: The Walmart/Costco selloffs are valuation victims of their own success — solid comps but multiples too high — and the broader tell is the consumer is not as healthy as the market assumes regardless of either company's specifics.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Reinforces that high-multiple defensives offer poor risk/reward when the consumer narrative turns.
insights.quote: they're losing to it in the form of valuation. They're sort of a victim of their own success

insights.claim: Dollar Tree (reported this week) and Dollar General (next week) are key reads on the low-end consumer, with Home Depot already disappointing, making the upcoming May jobs report (June 5) a critical near-term catalyst amid retail crosscurrents.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Dollar-store guidance plus the May payrolls print are the next datapoints to confirm or refute the weak-consumer thesis.

insights.claim: The economy is a 'K' where the upper-income consumer keeps spending on the wealth effect from record equities and a locked, high-value housing market with low legacy mortgage rates, while the lower part is strapped — a bifurcation propping up aggregate spending.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: The bull case rests on high-end wealth-effect spending, which is itself hostage to equity prices — a reflexive vulnerability if markets fall.
insights.quote: it's that higher earning consumer that continues to spend... when you think about the wealth effect from the stock market... that's keeping that upper part of the K going.

insights.claim: Valuation extremes are flashing red: the Buffett indicator (market cap to GDP) is at an all-time high and the CAPE (cyclically adjusted P/E) is at its second-highest reading in history (data back to the late 1800s) — though the hosts stress valuation is not a timing tool.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Record long-horizon valuation metrics imply poor forward returns even if they can't time the top.
insights.quote: the cape ratio, the cyclically adjusted price to earnings is the second highest reading in history.

insights.claim: Guy Adami's explicit call: the S&P and NASDAQ will be cut in half at some point in the next one to two years, and this euphoric price action has never historically ended well — 'it is not going to be different this time.'
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: A concrete, high-conviction drawdown call (~50%) that argues for active risk management in euphoric conditions.
insights.quote: The stock market, the S&P and the NASDAQ are going to get cut in half at some point in the next year or two.

insights.claim: The hosts reject the 'irrational exuberance' bull rebuttal — that Greenspan's Dec 1996 warning preceded four more years of ~25%/yr gains and an 85% NASDAQ surge in 1999 — arguing current conditions are unusually extreme and everyone is simply afraid to call the top after being burned in 1998 and 2006.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Counters the 'bubbles can run for years' complacency with a claim that the current setup is qualitatively more dangerous.
insights.quote: what's happened here, make no mistake about it, is very, very unusual. It will not end well.

insights.claim: Concentration risk is acute: 30-40% of the biggest names sit in the ETFs/indices held in ordinary retirement accounts, so a mega-cap unwind would hit passive/retirement investors disproportionately.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Index concentration means passive holders carry hidden single-stock-like risk into any mega-cap drawdown.
insights.quote: because of the 30 and 40% of these biggest names in the ETFs or the indices that you own in your retirement

insights.claim: Today's exuberance is 'eerily reminiscent' of conditions roughly a year before the 1929 crash, per Andrew Ross Sorkin's book '1929'; while not a definitive full-blown-crash call, the hosts say history is clearly rhyming with the 1920s-30s.
insights.speaker: Guy Adami
insights.so_what: Adds a historical-analog frame reinforcing late-cycle caution, useful as a sentiment/positioning tell.
insights.quote: the excitement, the exuberance people feel today are eerily reminiscent of the things people felt... a year or so prior to one of the darkest periods in our history.

insights.claim: Dan Loeb said on Patrick O'Shaughnessy's podcast he is reviewing hedge-fund portfolios' semiconductor exposure and 'can't think of a better time to remain long' semis — but the hosts caveat he is also short other names and manages risk in ways retail cannot replicate.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: A prominent bullish semi soundbite is being amplified out of its risk-managed, long/short context — a caution against copying headline positioning.
insights.quote: he's talking about... how exposed they are to semis and he can't think of a better time to remain long them.
